meta.title: MergeCore Python Rules
meta.description: Rules for production Python: typing, exceptions, concurrency, resource management, and safe execution.
meta.notes: Gate with python: true. Rules assume Python 3.10+; flag version-dependent findings explicitly.

scoring.normalisation: Per review unit (one module), sum penalties after optional quality_signal multiplier, then score = max(0, min(10, initial_score - min(sum_penalties, max_total_penalty_per_file))).

interpretation.9_to_10: Typed, idiomatic, safe I/O, explicit errors.
interpretation.7_to_8: Mergeable; tighten exception handling or typing at boundaries.
interpretation.5_to_6: Risky: bare except, mutable defaults, or unbounded I/O.
interpretation.0_to_4: Block: shell injection, eval on untrusted input, or leaked secrets.

rules.title: eval/exec on untrusted input
rules.description: eval(), exec(), and compile() on user or remote input enable remote code execution. Use explicit parsing (ast.literal_eval, json.loads) or a domain-specific parser.

rules.title: subprocess with shell=True and interpolated input
rules.description: shell=True with string interpolation is shell injection. Pass an argv list and shell=False (default) or quote with shlex.

rules.title: Mutable default argument
rules.description: Default arguments are evaluated once at function definition; using [] or {} as defaults shares state across calls.

prefer: None sentinel, assign inside the body

examples.before: def add(item, bucket=[]): bucket.append(item); return bucket
examples.after: def add(item, bucket=None): bucket = [] if bucket is None else bucket; bucket.append(item); return bucket

rules.title: Bare except or except Exception swallowing errors
rules.description: Catching every exception and logging or passing hides real defects and breaks KeyboardInterrupt/SystemExit. Catch specific types; reraise where appropriate.

rules.title: File or connection opened without a context manager
rules.description: open(), socket(), and database connections should use with-blocks or explicit try/finally to release handles.

examples.after: with open(path) as f: data = f.read()

rules.title: Public function missing type hints
rules.description: Public callables benefit from signatures. Type hints aid IDEs, mypy/pyright, and runtime validation.

rules.title: Blocking call inside async function
rules.description: time.sleep, requests.get, or synchronous DB drivers inside async def stall the event loop. Use the async equivalent or run_in_executor.

rules.title: Classes used where a function or dataclass would do
rules.description: Thin wrapper classes with __init__ storing attributes and one method are typically better modelled as functions or dataclasses.

heuristics: class with __init__ + one method, no state evolution

rules.title: Wildcard import pollutes namespace
rules.description: from module import * imports hide origins and break static analysis. Import names explicitly.

rules.title: Secrets or tokens leaking to logs
rules.description: Logging full request bodies, auth headers, or tokens breaches compliance and aids attackers. Redact or structure logging with allow-lists.

rules.title: Strong pattern: domain-specific exception types
rules.description: Custom exception hierarchies communicate intent and enable precise handling at call sites.

rules.title: String concatenation in a loop
rules.description: Repeated += on strings creates O(n^2) allocations. Use str.join or write to a buffer.
